title: Design System Documentation Spec (DSDS) v0.2.1 — Bundled
description: Single-file bundled version of the DSDS schema. Auto-generated from the split schema files by scripts/bundle.js. Use the split files for development; use this file for tools that require a single schema document.

$schema.description: URI reference to the DSDS JSON Schema for validation.

dsdsVersion.description: The DSDS spec version this document conforms to.

extends.description: Declares that this DSDS document inherits from another DSDS document — typically a core design system. The base document provides the foundational entities. This document layers on top, adding or extending entities. Enables systems-of-systems architectures where product- or brand-specific systems inherit from a shared core.

purpose.description: The purpose of the design system as a whole — why it exists, what problems it solves, and who it serves. Helps stakeholders, new team members, and external consumers understand the system's intent before exploring individual components or tokens.

description.description: A high-level description of the design system's purpose and the problems it solves.

useCases.description: Scenario-driven guidance describing when this design system is or is not the right choice. Positive cases describe when the system should be adopted. Negative cases describe when a different approach fits better, and why.

bestPractices.description: System-level best practices that apply across the entire design system — broad guidance for teams adopting, contributing to, or consuming the system. These are not component-specific rules (those live in each entity's guidelines array) but cross-cutting principles like 'always use semantic tokens instead of raw values' or 'test all components at 200% zoom'. Each entry is a self-contained rule paired with a rationale.

documentation.description: One or more documentation groups. Each group is a named collection that organizes entities into separate typed arrays — components, tokenGroups, themes, styles, and patterns. Multiple groups let a single DSDS file organize entities into logical sections (e.g., one group for foundations, another for components). All entity arrays are optional; a group only needs a name. Items may be inlined or referenced via a $ref object pointing to another DSDS file.

entity.description: A single entity document — one component, token, token group, theme, style, or pattern. Use this instead of `documentation` when each entity lives in its own file. The `kind` field on the entity identifies its type.

agents.description: AI-ready context for a design system entity. Each property is structured for direct machine use: constraints carry explicit levels, disambiguation resolves common confusion with similar entities, anti-patterns pair bad practices with correct alternatives, examples give ready-to-use code, and keywords support semantic retrieval.

intent.description: A single sentence stating the primary purpose of this entity: what problem it solves and why it exists. MUST be concrete and distinct from similar entities. Agents use it to judge relevance quickly without parsing the full description.

constraints.description: Hard rules for using this entity. Each constraint pairs a rule with an enforcement level aligned to RFC 2119. Agents use these to validate decisions without inferring rules from prose.

rule.description: The constraint statement. MUST be concrete, unambiguous, and actionable. Avoid 'use sparingly' or 'when appropriate'; state exact conditions.

level.description: Enforcement level. 'must' and 'must-not' are hard constraints; violations are defects. 'should' and 'should-not' are strong recommendations; exceptions need justification.

context.description: Optional scope, condition, or rationale for this constraint. Use it to say when the rule applies (e.g., 'when used inside a Dialog', 'for mobile viewports') and why it matters. Omit when the constraint applies universally and the reason is self-evident.

evidence.description: Quantitative or empirical backing for this constraint: test run data, audit findings, issue references, or cited sources. Keeping evidence separate from context lets tools tell test-derived rules apart from conventional guidance. Example: '9/10 agent test runs failed touch-target minimums when height was overridden below 36px.'

examples.description: Inline examples that show this specific constraint. Use them for before/after pairs that illustrate the rule directly. Prefer these over top-level examples when the code ties tightly to one rule.

description.description: The scenario or use case this example demonstrates.

code.description: The code or markup for this example. SHOULD be immediately usable with minimal modification.

language.description: The language or format of the code (e.g., 'html', 'jsx', 'tsx', 'css', 'json'). Omit when the language is obvious from context.

disambiguation.description: Entries that resolve common confusion between this entity and similar ones. Each entry names a confusable entity and states the precise distinction. Agents use it to pick the right entity without trial and error.

entity.description: The name of the similar or commonly confused entity (e.g., 'button', 'link', 'chip').

entityType.description: The kind of entity being disambiguated. Lets tools filter or group disambiguations (e.g., show only component-level distinctions). Omit when the type is obvious from context.

distinction.description: A concrete statement of what sets this entity apart from the named entity. MUST state the deciding criterion, not just describe each entity on its own.

antiPatterns.description: Common incorrect uses of this entity. Each entry describes what NOT to do and provides the correct alternative. Every entry SHOULD include an `instead` value — an anti-pattern without a correction is not actionable guidance. Agents use these to avoid known failure modes.

description.description: What the anti-pattern looks like — the incorrect usage to avoid.

instead.description: What to do instead. SHOULD reference a concrete alternative (another entity name, a correct pattern, or a specific approach). Omitting this field is discouraged — an anti-pattern without a fix is an accusation without a remedy.

evidence.description: Quantitative or empirical backing for this anti-pattern — test failure rates, bug report references, or audit data. Example: 'Raised in 10/10 agent iterations when generating form layouts.'

examples.description: Usage examples agents can apply directly. Each example pairs a description of the scenario with code or a structured representation. Prefer minimal, correct examples over exhaustive ones. For examples tied to a specific constraint, use the constraint-level examples property instead.

description.description: The scenario or use case this example demonstrates.

code.description: The code or markup for this example. SHOULD be immediately usable with minimal modification.

language.description: The language or format of the code (e.g., 'html', 'jsx', 'tsx', 'css', 'json'). Omit when the language is obvious from context.

keywords.description: Semantic terms that support retrieval and entity matching. Include synonyms, related concepts, common query terms, and vocabulary that designers or engineers use when searching for this entity but that may not appear in its name or description.

verified.description: ISO date when this agent context block was last validated against the live design system. Absence signals unverified content. Agents and tooling can use this to assess data freshness and flag stale guidance.

verifiedAgainst.description: The package version, test run identifier, or audit reference this block was validated against. Provides traceability for empirical data. Example: '@sanity/ui@3.2.0', 'agent-test-run-2026-05-30', 'a11y-audit-Q2-2026'.

example.description: A single example. Requires at least a presentation or a value (or both). The presentation shows a visual or interactive demo. The value carries a literal for API property examples. Optional title and description add context. When only a value is present, the example represents a data point (e.g., a prop value of 'primary' or a token value of 44). Tools SHOULD render value-only examples as table rows or inline displays.

title.description: A human-readable caption for the example (e.g., 'Primary button in default state'). For API property examples, use `value` for the literal content and `title` for the display label.

description.description: An explanation of the example — why it is encouraged, discouraged, or notable.

value.description: A literal value carried by the example. Used for API property examples where the content is a concrete value rather than a visual demo. Can display code that reflects the example. Accepts any JSON type: string, number, boolean, object, array, or null. When provided without a presentation, this value alone is the example. Do not use `value` for display text — use `title` for captions and `description` for explanations.

presentation.description: The visual or interactive presentation for the example. Required when no `value` is set. Optional when `value` is present — value-only examples represent concrete data points that tools render as table rows or inline displays.

documentExtends.description: Says that this DSDS document inherits from another DSDS document. The base document supplies the core entities: components, tokens, themes, styles, and patterns. This document layers on top, adding new entities or extending base entities. The declaration sets up the relationship; the consuming tools handle merge and resolution. A common case: a product-specific system that inherits from an organization's core design system.

system.description: The name or identifier of the base design system being extended (e.g., 'Acme Core Design System', 'acme-core', '@acme/design-system'). A display name or a package name, not a URL. Tools use it to resolve the base system from a registry, file system, or package manager.

url.description: URL to the base DSDS document (e.g., 'https://design.acme.com/v2/core.dsds.json', './core.dsds.json'). When provided, tools MAY fetch and resolve the base document for merge, validation, or docs generation.

version.description: The version of the base system this document extends (e.g., '2.0.0', '>=2.0.0', '^3.1.0'). Follows semver. When omitted, tools SHOULD resolve to the latest available version.

description.description: Description of the relationship: what this extension adds, changes, or narrows relative to the base system.

entityExtends.description: Says that this entity inherits from a base entity in a parent design system. The base entity supplies the foundational definition: identity, anatomy, API, variants, states, guidelines, and so on. This entity layers on top, adding properties, guidelines, or specialized behavior. For example, an enterprise Button might extend the core Button to add variants, accessibility requirements, or API props, while inheriting the core's anatomy, states, and base guidelines. The declaration sets up the relationship; the consuming tools handle merge and resolution.

identifier.description: The identifier of the base entity being extended (e.g., 'button', 'color-text-primary', 'form-validation'). MUST match the `identifier` property of the base entity in the parent system.

system.description: The name or identifier of the system that owns the base entity (e.g., 'Acme Core Design System', 'acme-core'). When omitted, the base entity is resolved from the system declared in the root document's `extends` property. When provided, lets you extend from a system other than the document's default base.

url.description: Direct URL to the base entity's docs or DSDS definition. Useful for cross-linking in docs sites.

version.description: The version of the base entity or base system this entity extends (e.g., '2.0.0'). When omitted, the version comes from the document-level extends declaration.

description.description: What this entity adds or changes relative to the base: new variants, more props, overridden guidelines, and so on.

modifications.description: Summary of what this entity changes relative to the base. Each entry describes one change. Tools MAY use these for changelog generation, diff views, or migration guides. When omitted, consuming tools must diff the entities to find changes.

type.description: The change type: 'added' (not in the base), 'modified' (changed from the base), 'removed' (in the base but dropped here), 'inherited' (carried from the base unchanged — usually omitted, since inheritance is the default, but useful for docs).

target.description: What changed: a property name, guideline kind, variant name, or other identifier (e.g., 'variant:enterprise', 'prop:theme', 'guideline:accessibility', 'state:read-only').

description.description: A short description of the change.

extensions.description: Vendor-specific extensions. Keys MUST use vendor-specific namespaces (reverse domain name notation recommended). Tools that do not recognize an extension MUST preserve it. Extension data SHOULD NOT duplicate core schema fields.

link.description: A typed pointer to an external resource or another DSDS artifact. Links point to external resources (source code, design files, docs, packages), express relationships to other DSDS artifacts by identifier, or combine both when an internal artifact also has a public URL. The `kind` property classifies the reference. Standard external kinds: 'source', 'design', 'storybook', 'documentation', 'package', 'repository'. Standard relationship kinds: 'alternative', 'parent', 'child', 'related'. Standard artifact kinds (for internal references): 'component', 'token', 'token-group', 'style', 'pattern', 'theme'. Custom values are allowed.

kind.description: The category of the linked resource or relationship. Standard external kinds: 'source', 'design', 'storybook', 'documentation', 'package', 'repository'. Standard relationship kinds: 'alternative', 'parent', 'child', 'related'. Standard artifact kinds (for internal references): 'component', 'token', 'token-group', 'style', 'pattern', 'theme'. Custom values are allowed and SHOULD match ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$.

url.description: The URL of the linked resource. MUST be a valid absolute URI. Required for external resource links. Optional for internal artifact references (where `identifier` names the artifact). When both `url` and `identifier` are present, `url` points to the artifact's docs page and `identifier` is the machine-readable cross-reference.

identifier.description: The identifier of a referenced DSDS artifact (e.g., 'button', 'form-field', 'color-text'). MUST match the `identifier` property of a documented DSDS artifact. Required for internal artifact references. Optional for external resource links. When present, tools SHOULD resolve this identifier against the artifact catalog to create navigable cross-references.

label.description: Display text for this link — what a user sees when the link renders (e.g., 'React component source', 'Design file — variants', 'Button component'). Use it for the display name of the resource. To describe an internal artifact's role, use `role` instead.

role.description: The functional role this artifact plays in the current entity (e.g., 'Primary action trigger', 'Error summary container', 'Provides semantic text color tokens'). Distinct from `label`, which is display text for the link itself. Use it on internal artifact references to explain *why* this artifact is linked. When omitted, the link is a general association.

required.description: Whether this linked artifact is required for the parent entity to work. Used on internal artifact references — for example, a form-validation pattern might mark 'form-field' as required and 'toast' as optional. Defaults to false. Not used on external resource links.

presentationImage.description: An image presentation — a screenshot, diagram, annotated mockup, or exported design frame.

kind.description: Identifies this presentation as an image.

url.description: URL or relative path to the image file.

alt.description: Alternative text describing the image content. MUST be provided for accessibility.

presentationVideo.description: A video presentation — a screen recording, animated transition, or interaction walkthrough.

kind.description: Identifies this presentation as a video.

url.description: URL or relative path to the video file.

alt.description: Alternative text describing the video content. MUST be provided for accessibility.

presentationCode.description: A code presentation — a source code snippet showing how to use the artifact.

kind.description: Identifies this presentation as a code snippet.

code.description: The source code of the example.

presentationUrl.description: A URL presentation — a link to a web resource such as a live demo, interactive example, docs page, or hosted component story. Use for Storybook stories, CodeSandbox embeds, StackBlitz projects, or other addressable web content.

kind.description: Identifies this presentation as a URL reference to a web resource.

useCase.description: A single use case scenario describing when an artifact or option is or is not appropriate.

description.description: Describes the scenario. Should be concrete and situation-driven — describe the user's situation, not the artifact's features.

kind.description: Whether the use case is an example to follow or to avoid.

alternative.description: The recommended alternative artifact for this scenario. Typically provided for whenNotToUse entries to direct the reader toward a more appropriate component, pattern, or token.

identifier.description: The identifier of the recommended alternative artifact (e.g., 'link', 'radio-group', 'icon-button', 'empty-state').

rationale.description: Why the alternative is more appropriate for this scenario. Cite semantic differences, accessibility implications, or UX rationale when available.

purpose.description: Documents the purpose of an artifact — when to use it and when to choose something else. Contains a `useCases` object with whenToUse and whenNotToUse arrays of scenario-driven entries from the common useCases schema. Purpose guidelines help the reader decide whether this is the right artifact *before* implementing it. For guidance on *how* to use it correctly, see best-practices. For *what* it looks like and how it behaves, see variants, states, and anatomy.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a purpose spec.

useCases.description: Scenario-driven guidance describing when this artifact is or is not the right choice.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

richText.description: Text field supporting multiple content formats. Accepts a plain string (defaults to markdown for backward compatibility) or a structured object with an explicit format.

oneOf.description: A string value. Bare strings default to markdown for backward compatibility.
A structured content object with an explicit format.

format.description: The content format. 'plain' is unformatted text. 'markdown' is CommonMark (0.27 minimum). 'html' is sanitized HTML. Defaults to 'markdown'.

statusValue.description: A lifecycle status value. MUST be lowercase kebab-case. Standard values: 'draft' (under development, not ready for use), 'experimental' (available, but the API may change without notice), 'stable' (production-ready; changes follow semver), 'deprecated' (scheduled for removal). Custom values are allowed (e.g., 'sunset', 'archived', 'beta') and MUST follow the same pattern.

platformStatus.description: Readiness of an artifact on one specific platform. Tracks lifecycle stage, the version where it became available, and an optional deprecation notice.

status.description: Lifecycle status of the artifact on this platform. Uses the same vocabulary as the top-level status: 'draft', 'experimental', 'stable', 'deprecated'. Custom values are allowed.

since.description: The version in which this artifact became available on this platform.

deprecationNotice.description: Required when status is 'deprecated'. Says what to use instead on this platform.

description.description: Notes about the artifact's status on this platform (e.g., 'Available as a Web Component wrapper; native implementation planned for v4').

statusObject.description: Full lifecycle status of an artifact. Holds the overall status value, optional per-platform readiness, and a conditional deprecation notice. Every status-bearing artifact uses this one object instead of separate status, platformStatus, and deprecationNotice siblings. When overall status is 'deprecated', deprecationNotice becomes required.

overall.description: Overall lifecycle status of the artifact. An editorial judgment of maturity; it need not match the most or least advanced platform status. Tools SHOULD display this as the primary status indicator.

platforms.description: Per-platform readiness. Keys are platform identifiers (freeform strings; common values include 'react', 'web-component', 'ios', 'android', 'flutter', 'figma', 'sketch', 'compose'). Values describe the artifact's status on that platform. An artifact MAY be 'stable' overall while individual platforms are still 'experimental' or 'draft'.

deprecationNotice.description: Required when status is 'deprecated'. MUST say what to use instead and give a migration path. Tools SHOULD display this prominently next to the status indicator.

status.description: Lifecycle status of an artifact. Accepts a plain status string (e.g., "stable", "deprecated") for the simple case, or a full status object with per-platform tracking and a deprecation notice. Tools SHOULD normalize the string form to { "overall": value } for consistent processing.

systemMetadata.description: System-level metadata about the design system.

systemName.description: The name of the design system.

systemVersion.description: The version of the design system.

organization.description: The organization that maintains the system.

url.description: URL to the system's documentation site.

license.description: SPDX license identifier or license URL.

items.description: Alternative names across teams, tools, or legacy systems.

value.description: A description of the entity. CommonMark supported.
ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) of the most recent update to this entity's documentation.

description.description: Optional plain-text note summarizing what changed in the most recent update (e.g., 'Added focus-visible guidance', 'Revised contrast requirements for inverse surfaces'). MUST NOT contain markup.

items.description: Links to source code, design files, related entities, etc.

deprecationNotice.description: Required when status is deprecated. MUST explain what to use instead.

value.description: One-line plain-text summary. MUST NOT contain markup.

items.description: Keywords for categorization, search, and cross-referencing.

keyboardInteraction.description: A keyboard interaction spec documenting what happens when a specific key or key combination is pressed.

key.description: The key or key combination (e.g., 'Enter', 'Space', 'Tab', 'Shift+Tab', 'Escape', 'Arrow Down', 'Home', 'End').

action.description: What happens when this key is pressed (e.g., 'Activates the button', 'Moves focus to the next item in the list', 'Closes the dialog and returns focus to the trigger').

context.description: Preconditions for this interaction (e.g., 'When the menu is open', 'When focus is on the trigger element', 'When the component is not disabled'). When omitted, the interaction applies in all contexts.

ariaAttribute.description: An ARIA attribute used by the artifact — what it communicates and when to apply it.

value.description: The expected value or value pattern (e.g., 'true | false', 'string', 'button', 'dialog', 'assertive | polite').

description.description: What this attribute communicates to assistive technology and when to apply it. Include guidance on dynamic value changes when applicable.

required.description: Whether this attribute is always required on the artifact. Defaults to false, meaning the attribute is conditionally required or optional.

colorContrast.description: A color contrast spec: the measured contrast ratio between a foreground and background color pair and the WCAG conformance level it meets.

foreground.description: The foreground token name or resolved color value (e.g., 'color-text-primary', '#1a1a1a', 'var(--button-text-color)').

background.description: The background token name or resolved color value (e.g., 'color-background-default', '#ffffff', 'var(--button-background)').

contrastRatio.description: The contrast ratio as a decimal (e.g., 4.5, 7.2, 15.3). Represents the ratio N:1 where N is this value. Tools MAY format for display as 'N:1'.

level.description: The WCAG conformance level met by this contrast ratio. AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text. AAA requires 7:1 for normal text and 4.5:1 for large text.

context.description: Where this foreground/background combination is used (e.g., 'Label text on primary button background in light mode', 'Secondary text on card surface').

accessibility.description: Structured accessibility specs for an artifact. Documents keyboard behavior, ARIA attributes, screen reader announcements, focus management, color contrast pairs, and motion considerations. Distinct from best-practice guidelines — accessibility guidelines capture machine-readable specs, while best practices capture actionable rules with rationale. Both can coexist in the same guidelines array.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as an accessibility spec.

wcagLevel.description: The minimum WCAG conformance level targeted by this artifact. Most design systems target AA. Set to AAA when the artifact exceeds standard requirements.

keyboardInteraction.description: Keyboard interaction specs — what happens when each key or key combination is pressed while the artifact has focus.

ariaAttributes.description: ARIA attribute docs. Lists every ARIA attribute the artifact uses, what it communicates, and when to apply it.

screenReaderBehavior.description: How the artifact is announced by screen readers in various states. Include announcements for default, focused, disabled, loading, expanded, and other relevant states. Name specific screen readers (VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS) when behavior differs.

focusManagement.description: How focus moves into, within, and out of the artifact. Document focus trapping (for modals/dialogs), focus restoration (when dismissing overlays), and programmatic focus movement. Describe both keyboard and programmatic focus behavior.

colorContrast.description: Contrast ratio documentation for foreground/background color pairs used by this artifact. Each entry documents a specific pairing, its measured ratio, and the WCAG level it meets.

motionConsiderations.description: How the artifact respects the prefers-reduced-motion media query. Document what animations or transitions exist and what happens when reduced motion is preferred (e.g., 'The loading spinner is replaced with a static ellipsis indicator').

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

anatomyEntry.description: A named sub-element of a component's or pattern's visual structure.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the part (e.g., 'container', 'label', 'icon', 'focus-ring').

description.description: What this part is, what it does, and any constraints on its content or appearance.

required.description: Whether this part is always present in the rendered output. Defaults to false. When false, the part is conditionally rendered based on props or content.

tokens.description: Design tokens applied to this part. Keys are token-purpose names describing what visual attribute the token controls (e.g., 'background', 'border-radius', 'padding-horizontal', 'text-color'). Values are the token identifiers that provide the value (e.g., 'color-action-primary', 'radius-md', 'space-4').

links.description: Links to resources for this anatomy part — design tool nodes, source code blocks, documentation sections, or other addressable references.

anatomy.description: Documents the visual structure of a component or pattern by listing its named sub-elements (parts). Each part can reference the design tokens that style it, bridging visual design and token architecture. An optional preview shows an annotated diagram of the assembled structure. For components, parts are rendered UI sub-elements (e.g., container, label, icon, focus-ring). For patterns, parts are structural sections of the layout (e.g., image, title, body, primary action).

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as an anatomy spec.

description.description: Prose overview of the artifact's visual structure — how the parts relate and any notable structural constraints.

parts.description: The named sub-elements of the artifact, in visual order (typically outside-in or top-to-bottom). Each part documents an element that can be styled, tested, or referenced independently.

preview.description: Visual previews of the anatomy — typically annotated diagrams with numbered callouts mapping to the parts array. Multiple previews allow different views (e.g., expanded vs. collapsed, with icon vs. without).

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

apiProperty.description: A configurable property of a component.

identifier.description: The property identifier as used in code (e.g., 'variant', 'size', 'disabled', 'onClick').

type.description: The property's type as it would appear in a type signature (e.g., 'boolean', 'number', 'string', 'ReactNode', 'IconComponent'). For enum properties, use `values` instead of encoding members here.

values.description: The accepted values for enum-like properties (e.g., ['default', 'primary', 'ghost']). Use this instead of encoding members in `type`.

schema.description: A JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12) object defining the property's type in machine-readable form. Follows the same conventions as OpenAPI parameter schemas.

description.description: What this property controls, how it affects the component, and any constraints or side effects.

required.description: Whether the consumer must provide this property. Defaults to false.

defaultValue.description: The default value in its native JSON type. Accepts any JSON type: string, number, boolean, object, array, or null.

since.description: The version in which this property was introduced.

deprecated.description: Whether this property is deprecated. Defaults to false.

deprecationNotice.description: What to use instead, if deprecated.

apiEvent.description: An event emitted by a component.

identifier.description: The event identifier (e.g., 'onClick', 'onChange', 'onDismiss').

description.description: When this event fires, what it communicates, and how consumers should respond.

returns.description: Description of the event return shape (e.g., '(event: MouseEvent) => void', '{ value: string, index: number }').

since.description: The version in which this event was introduced.

apiSlot.description: A content insertion point of a component.

identifier.description: The slot identifier. Use 'default' for the unnamed/default slot.

description.description: What content this slot accepts and any constraints on its children.

acceptedContent.description: Allowed content (e.g., 'Plain text or a text node. Do not nest interactive elements.').

since.description: The version in which this slot was introduced.

apiCssCustomProperty.description: A CSS custom property exposed by a component for external styling.

identifier.description: The full custom-property identifier including the -- prefix (e.g., '--button-background', '--button-border-radius').

description.description: What visual attribute this property controls.

since.description: The version in which this custom property was introduced.

apiCssPart.description: A CSS shadow part exposed by a web component for external styling via ::part() selectors.

identifier.description: The part identifier as used in ::part() selectors.

description.description: What element this part exposes and what styling it enables.

since.description: The version in which this part was introduced.

apiDataAttribute.description: A data attribute used by a component to reflect state or variant information into the DOM.

identifier.description: The full attribute identifier including the data- prefix (e.g., 'data-state', 'data-variant').

description.description: What this attribute reflects and how to use it for styling or testing.

since.description: The version in which this attribute was introduced.

apiMethod.description: A public method on a component instance.

identifier.description: The method identifier (e.g., 'focus', 'reset', 'scrollTo').

description.description: What this method does, when to call it, and any side effects.

parameters.description: The parameter signature (e.g., '(options?: { preventScroll: boolean }) => void').

since.description: The version in which this method was introduced.

api.description: Documents the code-level interface of a component on a single platform. Lists configurable properties, emitted events, content slots, CSS hooks, data attributes, and public methods. For multi-platform components (e.g., React + Web Component + Vue), create one API guideline per platform and use `platform` to distinguish them.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as an API spec.

platform.description: The platform or framework this API describes (e.g., 'react', 'web-component', 'vue', 'angular', 'ios', 'android'). When omitted, the API is assumed to be the single/default platform.

properties.description: Configurable properties (props, attributes, inputs) that consumers pass to the component.

events.description: Events emitted by the component that consumers can listen to.

slots.description: Content insertion points where consumers can project child content.

cssCustomProperties.description: CSS custom properties exposed for external styling.

cssParts.description: CSS shadow parts exposed for external styling via ::part() selectors.

dataAttributes.description: Data attributes that reflect component state or config into the DOM.

methods.description: Public methods on the component instance.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

contentLabelEntry.description: A single recommended action label or term in the content dictionary. Each entry sets a standard label, its meaning, usage guidance, and cross-references to related or alternative terms. Entries follow the {verb} + {noun} content formula used by systems like Carbon and Gestalt.

term.description: The recommended label text (e.g., 'Add', 'Cancel', 'Delete', 'Save as', 'Learn more'). The canonical form as it should appear in the UI.

definition.description: What this label means — the action it represents. Should be concrete and unambiguous (e.g., for Delete: 'Destroys an existing object so that it no longer exists').

usage.description: When and how to use this label. Include context, formatting rules, and any caveats (e.g., 'Where appropriate, combine Add with the object: Add user, Add role').

alternatives.description: Related terms that serve a different purpose but are often confused with this one. Listed for comparison; helps authors pick the right label. The terms SHOULD match other `term` values in the same content guideline so tools can cross-reference them.

context.description: Where this label is used in the UI (e.g., 'Buttons in dialogs', 'Navigation links', 'Form submission', 'Toolbar actions'). Tells authors where the label normally appears.

examples.description: Visual or code examples showing the label in context: do/don't pairs, screenshots of correct usage, or code snippets.

localizationEntry.description: A single localization or i18n concern. Each entry covers something that affects how content is authored, translated, or rendered across languages and writing systems.

concern.description: The localization concern being addressed. Standard values: 'rtl' (right-to-left layout mirroring and bidirectional text), 'text-expansion' (content lengthening during translation), 'pluralization' (plural forms varying across languages), 'date-format' (date and time formatting conventions), 'number-format' (decimal separators, grouping), 'currency' (currency symbol placement and formatting), 'text-direction' (mixed-direction content within a single string), 'icon-direction' (icons that imply directionality), 'truncation' (text overflow behavior across languages), 'sorting' (locale-specific sort order and collation), 'concatenation' (avoiding string concatenation that breaks in other languages). Custom values are allowed and SHOULD be lowercase kebab-case.

description.description: Guidance for handling this localization concern. Should be actionable and specific: say what changes across locales, what the author or developer must do, and what to avoid.

examples.description: Visual or code examples that illustrate the concern: before/after screenshots for RTL, samples of text expansion, or code patterns for pluralization.

content.description: Structured content guidelines: recommended action labels and localization considerations. The labels array is a dictionary of standard UI labels with definitions, usage context, and cross-references. The localization array covers i18n concerns like RTL behavior, text expansion, and pluralization. At least one of `labels` or `localization` is required. Content guidelines complement best-practice guidelines: best practices say 'Use sentence case' (a rule); content guidelines say 'Add: Takes an existing object and uses it in a new context' (a reference).

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a content spec.

description.description: Overview of the content guidelines: content philosophy, voice and tone principles, or the scope of labels and localization rules covered.

labels.description: Recommended action labels and terminology. Each entry sets a standard label, its meaning, usage guidance, and cross-references to related terms. Order matters: tools SHOULD preserve it for display. Authors MAY order alphabetically or by frequency of use.

localization.description: Localization and i18n considerations. Each entry covers a concern that affects content across languages and writing systems: RTL mirroring, text expansion during translation, pluralization rules, and other i18n concerns. Order matters: tools SHOULD display entries in this order. Authors SHOULD order by impact or frequency.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

designValue.description: A design value — either a design token name (e.g., 'color-action-primary', 'space-4') or a raw CSS value (e.g., '#0055b3', '16px', '0.4'). Systems with tokens reference token names; systems without tokens use raw values. Both are valid.

designProperties.description: A map of design property names to their values. Keys are component-scoped property names describing what the value controls (e.g., 'background', 'border-color', 'min-height', 'font-size', 'opacity'). Values are design token names or raw CSS values. Token-based systems use values like 'color-action-primary'. Non-token systems use values like '#0055b3' or '16px'. Both approaches — and mixing them — are supported.

spacingSpec.description: Spacing specs for the spatial relationships within and around the component. Separates internal spacing (owned by the component) from external spacing (recommended by the component but controlled by the parent layout). Values are design token names or raw CSS values.

internal.description: Padding and internal gaps within the component. Keys are named spatial relationships between anatomy parts or edges (e.g., 'container-horizontal', 'icon-to-label'). Values are token names or raw CSS values (e.g., 'space-4', '8px', '1rem').

external.description: Recommended margins and gaps between this component and siblings or container edges. Keys are named spatial relationships (e.g., 'button-to-button', 'button-group-gap', 'stack-gap'). Values are token names or raw CSS values. External spacing is a recommendation — the parent layout controls the space between components.

sizingSpec.description: Dimension constraints defining the min/max bounds of the component and any aspect ratio requirements. All values are token names or raw CSS values.

minWidth.description: Minimum width as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'size-button-min-width', '64px').

maxWidth.description: Maximum width as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'size-dialog-max-width', '600px', 'none').

minHeight.description: Minimum height as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'size-button-min-height', '44px').

maxHeight.description: Maximum height as a token name or raw value.

aspectRatio.description: Aspect ratio constraint as a ratio string (e.g., '16/9', '1/1', '4/3').

typographyEntrySpec.description: Typographic settings for a single text element within the component. Maps CSS typographic properties to token names or raw values. When a composite typeToken is provided, the individual properties document what the token resolves to.

fontFamily.description: Font family as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'font-family-body', 'Inter, sans-serif').

fontSize.description: Font size as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'font-size-body', '14px', '0.875rem').

fontWeight.description: Font weight as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'font-weight-regular', '400', 'bold').

lineHeight.description: Line height as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'line-height-body', '1.5', '20px').

letterSpacing.description: Letter spacing as a token name or raw value (e.g., 'letter-spacing-body', '0.01em').

typeToken.description: A composite typography token covering multiple typographic properties (e.g., '$body-compact-01', 'type-scale-body-sm'). When provided, individual properties (fontSize, fontWeight, etc.) document what the token resolves to.

typographySpec.description: Typographic specs for text content within the component. Keys are named text elements matching anatomy parts (e.g., 'label', 'helper-text', 'heading', 'body'). Values are typographyEntrySpec objects.

responsiveEntry.description: A responsive behavior spec describing how the component adapts at a specific breakpoint or viewport condition.

breakpoint.description: The breakpoint name or value at which this behavior applies (e.g., 'small', 'medium', 'large', '768px', 'min-width: 1024px').

description.description: How the component's layout, sizing, or behavior changes at this breakpoint.

specVariant.description: The full set of design properties for a single variant (e.g., 'primary', 'secondary', 'ghost', 'danger'). Each variant defines its own property-to-value mappings. Values are design token identifiers or raw CSS values. Systems with variant-specific tokens (like 'button-primary-background') use those directly — this is not an override of a base token, it is the variant's own token set.

identifier.description: The variant identifier. Must match a value defined in the component's variants guideline (e.g., 'primary', 'secondary', 'ghost', 'danger').

description.description: How this variant's visual treatment differs.

properties.description: Design properties for this variant. Keys are property names (e.g., 'background', 'text-color', 'border-color'). Values are token identifiers or raw CSS values.

spacing.description: Spacing adjustments for this variant, if any.

sizing.description: Sizing adjustments for this variant, if any.

typography.description: Typography adjustments for this variant, if any.

specSize.description: The full set of design properties for a single size (e.g., 'small', 'medium', 'large'). Each size defines its own property-to-value mappings for dimensions, spacing, typography, and other properties that scale with size.

identifier.description: The size identifier. Must match a value defined in the component's variants guideline (e.g., 'small', 'medium', 'large', 'sm', 'md', 'lg').

description.description: This size's intended use.

properties.description: Design properties for this size. Keys are property names (e.g., 'min-height', 'font-size', 'icon-size', 'padding-horizontal'). Values are token identifiers or raw CSS values.

specState.description: The full set of design properties for a single interactive state (e.g., 'hover', 'focus', 'disabled'). Each state defines its own property-to-value mappings. Values may reference state-specific tokens (e.g., 'button-hover-bg') or raw values (e.g., 'rgba(0,0,0,0.08)').

identifier.description: The state identifier. Must match a value defined in the component's states guideline (e.g., 'hover', 'active', 'focus', 'disabled', 'loading').

description.description: How the component changes in this state.

properties.description: Design properties for this state. Only properties that change from the default need to be listed. Keys are property names (e.g., 'background', 'opacity', 'focus-ring-color'). Values are token identifiers or raw CSS values.

spacing.description: Spacing adjustments for this state, if any.

sizing.description: Sizing adjustments for this state, if any.

typography.description: Typography adjustments for this state, if any.

specVariantState.description: Design properties for a specific variant–state combination (e.g., 'primary' + 'hover', 'danger' + 'active'). Used when a state's visual treatment differs across variants — for example, a primary button darkens its fill on hover while a ghost button gains a subtle background. Values may reference variant-state-specific tokens (e.g., 'button-primary-hover-bg') or raw values.

description.description: How this variant behaves in this state.

properties.description: Design properties for this variant–state combination.

designSpecifications.description: Documents the measurable visual specs of a component — every design property, spacing relationship, dimension constraint, typographic setting, and responsive adaptation. The base-level `properties` map defines the default spec. The `variants`, `sizes`, and `states` arrays group design properties by the condition they apply to. Each group entry is a self-contained set of property-to-value mappings — not an override of a base value. Values are design token names or raw CSS values.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a design specs document.

properties.description: Base design properties for the default config. Keys are property names describing what each value controls (e.g., 'background', 'text-color', 'border-radius'). Values are design token names or raw CSS values.

spacing.description: Base spacing spec for the default config.

sizing.description: Base sizing spec for the default config.

typography.description: Base typography spec for the default config.

responsive.description: Responsive behavior describing how the component adapts across breakpoints. Entries are ordered from smallest to largest breakpoint.

variants.description: Design properties grouped by variant. Each entry is a full set of property-to-value mappings for one variant. Values are the variant's own tokens (e.g., 'button-primary-bg') or raw values — not overrides of base tokens.

sizes.description: Design properties grouped by size. Each entry defines the dimensions, spacing, typography, and other properties that change at each size step.

states.description: Design properties grouped by interactive state. Each entry defines properties that change when the component enters a given state. Only properties that differ from the default need to be listed.

variantStates.description: Design properties for specific variant–state combinations where the state's visual treatment differs across variants. Only needed when a single state entry cannot describe the behavior across all variants.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

generalDocumentBlock.description: A document block kind available on every artifact type. General blocks cover cross-cutting concerns that apply to any artifact.

oneOf.description: Actionable usage rules paired with rationale. Tells the reader what to do (or not do) and why.
Scenario-driven guidance for when to use and when not to use the artifact.
Structured accessibility specs: keyboard interactions, ARIA attributes, screen reader behavior, focus management, color contrast, and motion considerations.
Structured content guidelines: recommended action labels with definitions, usage context, and cross-references, plus localization and i18n considerations.

componentDocumentBlock.description: A document block kind accepted on component artifacts. Includes the component-specific kinds (anatomy, API, variants, states, design specs) plus every general kind.

oneOf.description: How to import the component across platforms — package names, import statements, and setup context.
The visual structure of the component: named sub-elements (parts), their design tokens, and optional annotated diagrams.

The code-level interface: properties, emitted events, content slots, CSS custom properties, CSS shadow parts, data attributes, and public methods. For multi-platform components, include one API block per platform.
Events emitted by the component: trigger conditions, return shapes, DOM behavior (bubbling, cancelability, composed), and lifecycle metadata.
All dimensions of visual or behavioral variation. Each axis (size, emphasis, and so on) carries its values, token overrides, previews, and purpose. Optional exclusions document invalid combinations.
All interactive states the component can enter (hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, and the like). Each state carries its triggers, token overrides, and previews.
Measurable visual specs: token inventory, spacing relationships, dimension constraints, typography settings, and responsive behavior.

styleDocumentBlock.description: A document block kind accepted on style artifacts. Includes the style-specific kinds (principles, scales, motion) plus every general kind.

oneOf.description: High-level principles that shape decisions for the visual domain: what the system believes and which trade-offs it accepts.
An ordered sequence of values forming a visual scale: a type scale, spacing scale, elevation scale, or similar progression. Each step references a design token.
Named easing curves with cubic-bezier functions (aligned with the DTCG cubicBezier type), recommended durations, and usage guidance for the motion system.

patternDocumentBlock.description: A document block kind accepted on pattern artifacts. Includes the pattern-specific kinds (interactions), shared structural kinds (anatomy, variants, states), and every general kind.

oneOf.description: The interaction flow of the pattern: an ordered sequence of steps describing triggers, system responses, and component behavior.
The visual structure of the pattern: named sub-elements (image, title, body, primary action, and so on), their design tokens, and optional annotated diagrams.
Dimensions of variation within the pattern: sub-types, modes, or configurations (e.g., 'no-data', 'user-action', 'error-management'). Optional exclusions document invalid combinations.
States the pattern can enter (populated, empty, loading, error, and so on). Each state carries its triggers, visual changes, and optional previews.
Events emitted during the pattern's interaction flow: trigger conditions, return shapes, and lifecycle metadata.

tokenDocumentBlock.description: A document block kind accepted on token and token group artifacts. Tokens use only the general kinds; they do not have component-specific, style-specific, or pattern-specific docs.

eventEntry.description: A single event emitted by a component. Documents the event identifier, what triggers it, the return shape consumers receive, DOM propagation behavior, and lifecycle metadata.

identifier.description: The event identifier as used in code (e.g., 'onClick', 'onChange', 'onDismiss', 'ds-select'). For custom events, use the full event identifier including any namespace prefix.

description.description: When this event fires, what it communicates, and how consumers should respond. Include conditions under which the event does NOT fire (e.g., 'Does not fire when disabled').

returns.description: Human-readable description of the event return shape (e.g., '(event: MouseEvent) => void', '{ value: string, index: number }', 'CustomEvent<{ selectedId: string }>').

returnsSchema.description: Machine-readable JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12) describing the event return or detail object. Enables tooling to generate type definitions and validate event handlers.

bubbles.description: Whether the event bubbles up through the DOM tree. Defaults to true. Set to false for events that only fire on the target element.

cancelable.description: Whether the event can be cancelled via preventDefault(). Defaults to false. When true, document what cancelling the event prevents.

composed.description: Whether the event crosses shadow DOM boundaries. Defaults to false. Relevant for web components using shadow DOM encapsulation.

since.description: The version in which this event was introduced.

deprecated.description: Whether this event is deprecated. Defaults to false.

deprecationNotice.description: What to use instead when this event is deprecated. Required context when deprecated is true.

examples.description: Usage examples showing how to listen for and handle this event.

events.description: Documents the events emitted by a component as a dedicated document block. Each item describes a single event — its name, trigger conditions, return shape, DOM behavior, and lifecycle metadata. Separating events from the API block gives tooling a clear, searchable unit for event documentation and allows cross-referencing between events and the states or interactions that trigger them.

kind.description: Identifies this document block as an events spec.

items.description: The events emitted by this component. Each item documents a single event with its identifier, description, return shape, DOM behavior, and lifecycle metadata.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

guidelineEntry.description: A single piece of usage guidance. Each guideline pairs a statement with a rationale. `kind` sets the enforcement level. `category` groups by discipline. `target` points to a specific anatomy part or content area. `criteria` is a polymorphic array: each entry is either an external reference (citing WCAG or a similar standard) or an internal criterion definition (a stable, testable success criterion owned by this design system, citable via a display code that tooling manages).

guidance.description: The guidance statement. MUST be concrete and unambiguous. Avoid vague phrases like 'use sparingly' or 'when possible'.

rationale.description: Why this guidance exists. Cite evidence, accessibility standards, or user research when available. MUST NOT restate the guidance.

kind.description: How strictly the guideline should be followed. Values align with RFC 2119: 'required' (MUST — non-compliance is a defect), 'encouraged' (SHOULD — follow in most cases; exceptions need justification), 'informational' (MAY — advisory only), 'discouraged' (SHOULD NOT — avoid unless justified), 'prohibited' (MUST NOT — violations are defects). Defaults to 'informational'.

category.description: The discipline this guideline belongs to. Used to group guidelines by concern. Standard values: 'visual-design' (color, typography, spacing, layout), 'interaction' (behavior, animation, transitions, gestures), 'accessibility' (inclusive design, assistive tech, WCAG), 'content' (text, imagery, tone, voice, labeling), 'motion' (animation timing, easing, reduced-motion), 'development' (implementation patterns, performance, code conventions). Custom values are allowed and SHOULD be lowercase kebab-case.

target.description: The anatomy part or content area this guideline applies to (e.g., 'label', 'icon', 'helper-text'). When omitted, the guideline applies to the whole artifact.

criteria.description: Criteria this guideline addresses or defines. Each entry is either an external reference (citing a published standard such as WCAG) or an internal criterion (defining a testable success condition owned by this design system). External references cite an authoritative external rule. Internal criteria support WCAG-style governance: a stable, author-owned `identifier` anchors the criterion across edits, and tooling can derive citable display codes from those identifiers via a persistent registry. Authors SHOULD attach internal criteria only to guidelines whose success condition is objectively verifiable.

examples.description: Examples showing encouraged and discouraged approaches.

tags.description: Freeform keywords that relate guidelines across categories and disciplines (e.g., 'rtl', 'localization', 'validation', 'contrast').

criterionReference.description: A reference to an external standard this guideline addresses. Use it for published standards (WCAG, MDN, platform docs, vendor compliance frameworks). Pair the URL with a clear label so the citation reads well in display.

kind.description: Identifies this entry as a reference to an external standard.

url.description: URL to the external standard or requirement (e.g., 'https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#contrast-minimum').

label.description: A display label for the citation (e.g., '1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)', 'WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size'). When omitted, tools MAY derive a label from the URL.

criterionDefinition.description: A testable success criterion defined by this design system. It sits beside the parent guideline's guidance as a stable, citable unit for governance. Each definition pairs an author-owned `identifier` (frozen once published) with a `title`, an objectively verifiable `successCriterion`, and optional techniques, failure modes, examples, and links to external standards. Tooling derives display codes (e.g., 'BTN-2.1') from the identifier via a persistent registry, so citations stay stable as guidelines are edited or reordered.

kind.description: Identifies this entry as an internal success criterion defined by this design system.

identifier.description: Stable, author-owned identifier scoped to the parent entity. MUST be lowercase kebab-case, MUST be unique within the parent entity, and MUST NOT change once published. Tooling SHOULD derive the display code (e.g., 'BTN-2.1', 'A11Y-1.4.3') from this identifier via a persistent registry. Changing the identifier breaks every citation that points at the criterion, so treat renames as deprecation plus replacement.

title.description: A short display title for the criterion (e.g., 'Touch Target Minimum', 'Single Primary Action'). Tools SHOULD render this beside the display code when surfacing the criterion in docs or audit output.

successCriterion.description: The testable statement of what success looks like. MUST be objectively verifiable by inspection, static analysis, or runtime measurement. Avoid vague language like 'feels right' or 'looks balanced'. Mirrors the role of a WCAG Success Criterion (e.g., 'The visual presentation of text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for large text, incidental text, and logotypes').

techniques.description: Ways to satisfy this criterion. Each entry is a richText description of a sufficient technique (e.g., 'Apply `min-height: 44px` to every interactive element', 'Use the `<Button>` component without overriding its default height'). Maps to WCAG's 'Sufficient Techniques' list.

failures.description: Conditions that count as a failure of this criterion. Each entry is a richText description of a known failure mode (e.g., 'Setting `height` below 44px on an interactive element', 'Removing `aria-label` from an icon-only button'). Maps to WCAG's 'Common Failures' list.

references.description: External standards this internal criterion aligns with or derives from (e.g., a matching WCAG success criterion, an internal accessibility audit, a platform guideline). Each entry mirrors the shape of an external `criterionReference`.

url.description: URL to the aligned external standard.

label.description: A display label for the aligned standard.

examples.description: Examples that illustrate the criterion. Typically a before/after pair: one passing and one failing implementation.

tags.description: Freeform keywords that relate criteria across guidelines (e.g., 'a11y', 'contrast', 'touch-target', 'governance').

guideline.description: Actionable usage rules for an artifact. Each item pairs a guidance statement with a rationale. Categorize by discipline, target a specific anatomy part, and link to external standards like WCAG. Guidelines complement other document block kinds: use-cases say *when* to use the artifact; guidelines say *how* to use it correctly.

items.description: The guideline rules. Each item is a guidance statement paired with a rationale. Order matters: tools SHOULD display guidelines in this order. Authors SHOULD order by importance or group by category.

kind.description: Identifies this as a guideline spec.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

importEntry.description: A single platform-specific import statement for a component. Pairs the platform identifier with the code needed to import and use the component.

platform.description: The platform or framework this import applies to (e.g., 'react', 'vue', 'angular', 'svelte', 'web-component', 'ios', 'android'). Use the same platform identifiers as the API document block.

package.description: The package or module to install (e.g., '@acme/ui', '@acme/ui-vue', 'AcmeUI'). Include the full package name as it appears in the package manager.

code.description: The import statement or code snippet. SHOULD be copy-pasteable and immediately usable. For multi-line snippets (e.g., install + import), use newlines.

language.description: The language or format of the code (e.g., 'tsx', 'js', 'vue', 'swift', 'kotlin'). Omit when obvious from the platform.

description.description: Additional context about this import — when to use this path over alternatives, version requirements, or setup prerequisites.

since.description: The version in which this import path became available.

import.description: Documents how to import a component across platforms. Each item is a platform-specific import entry with the code needed to use the component. For components available on multiple platforms, include one entry per platform. Ordering is significant — list the primary/recommended platform first.

kind.description: Identifies this document block as an import spec.

items.description: Platform-specific import entries. Each item documents how to import the component on a specific platform or framework. List the primary/recommended platform first.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

interactionEntry.description: A single step in an interaction flow. Each interaction describes a trigger (what starts the step), a description (what happens), the components involved, and optional examples.

trigger.description: What starts this interaction step — a user action, system event, or condition (e.g., 'User submits the form by activating the submit button', 'API returns a 422 response'). When omitted, the step continues the previous step's flow without a new trigger.

description.description: What happens during this step — the system's response, visual changes, state transitions, and user-facing feedback. CommonMark supported.

components.description: Names of the components involved in this step. Each name MUST match the `name` of a component documented elsewhere in the system. Creates a cross-reference between the pattern's interaction flow and individual component docs.

examples.description: Visual or interactive examples for this step — screenshots of the resulting state, screen recordings of the transition, or code snippets showing the implementation.

interactions.description: Documents the interaction flow of a pattern as an ordered sequence of steps. Each item describes a single step — what triggers it, what the system does in response, which components are involved, and optional examples. Item ordering is critical: it represents the chronological flow of the user journey. Authors SHOULD include steps for the initial trigger, the system's response, recovery or correction steps, and completion. Multiple interaction guidelines on the same pattern can document alternative flows (e.g., a happy path and an error path).

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as an interactions spec.

items.description: The interaction steps in chronological order. Each item documents a single step with its trigger, description, involved components, and optional examples. Ordering is significant — tools SHOULD preserve and display steps in this order to represent the temporal sequence.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

motionDuration.description: A recommended duration range for an easing curve. Duration values are strings representing time values (e.g., '200ms', '0.3s') or token references (e.g., 'duration-quick'). When both min and max are provided, they define a range. When only one is provided, it serves as a point recommendation. When neither is provided, the description field should explain the guidance.

min.description: Minimum recommended duration as a time value or token reference (e.g., '200ms', '0.3s', 'duration-quick'). When omitted, there is no minimum recommendation.

max.description: Maximum recommended duration as a time value or token reference (e.g., '500ms', '0.5s', 'duration-slow'). When omitted, there is no maximum recommendation.

description.description: Freeform duration guidance. Use when the recommendation cannot be expressed as a min/max range (e.g., 'Depends on element size — use shorter durations for small elements and longer durations for full-screen transitions'). Also useful for supplementary notes when min/max are provided.

motionEntry.description: A single named easing in the motion system. Documents a cubic-bezier timing function with its recommended usage and duration. The `function` property uses the same four-number array format as the W3C Design Tokens Format Module cubicBezier type: [P1x, P1y, P2x, P2y] where P1x and P2x are in [0, 1] and P1y and P2y can be any real number.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier of the easing (e.g., 'expressive', 'enter', 'exit', 'lateral', 'bounce', 'linear'). Used for cross-referencing and tooling.

name.description: Human-readable name of the easing (e.g., 'Expressive Ease', 'Enter Ease', 'Exit Ease').

description.description: What this easing is for, when to use it, and how it affects the user experience. Explain the visual character of the curve and the types of transitions it suits.

function.description: Cubic-bezier control points as a four-number array [P1x, P1y, P2x, P2y], aligned with the W3C Design Tokens Format Module cubicBezier type (§8.6). P1x and P2x MUST be in [0, 1]. P1y and P2y can be any real number (values outside [0, 1] produce bounce/overshoot effects). Maps to CSS cubic-bezier() and DTCG cubicBezier token values. When omitted, the easing is described in the description field or references a token via `token`.

token.description: Reference to a DTCG cubicBezier or transition token that defines this easing (e.g., 'motion-ease-expressive', 'transition-enter'). When both `function` and `token` are present, `function` is the display value and `token` is the authoritative source.

duration.description: Recommended duration range when using this easing. Different easings suit different durations — enter transitions are typically longer (300–500ms) to draw attention, while exit transitions are shorter (200–300ms) to feel responsive.

usage.description: A short, comma-separated list of usage contexts (e.g., 'entering elements', 'exiting elements', 'lateral navigation', 'micro-interactions'). Provides a scannable summary — use description for detailed guidance.

examples.description: Visual or interactive examples showing this easing in action — screen recordings, interactive curve visualizations, or CSS code snippets.

motion.description: Documents the motion system for a visual style — named easing curves, timing functions, recommended durations, and usage guidance. Each item defines an easing that designers and engineers select when adding animation or transitions. Ordering is significant — tools SHOULD display easings in this order. A typical motion guideline lists easings from most common to most specialized. Scoped to style entities; complements the scale guideline (for duration token progressions) and best-practice guidelines (for motion rules like reduced-motion handling).

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a motion spec.

description.description: An overview of the motion system — the philosophy behind easing choices, how duration and easing interact, and system-wide motion principles not covered by individual easing entries.

items.description: Named easing definitions in the motion system. Each item documents a single easing curve with its cubic-bezier function, recommended duration, and usage guidance. Ordering is significant — tools SHOULD display easings in this order.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

principleEntry.description: A single guiding principle. Each principle has a short title and a description explaining what it means in practice and how it guides decisions. Principles are high-level — they set direction rather than prescribe specific actions. For specific, actionable rules, use best-practice guidelines instead.

title.description: A short, memorable name for the principle (e.g., 'Functional first', 'Accessible by default', 'Use the scale', 'Density over decoration'). Should be concise enough to serve as a heading or reference label.

description.description: What this principle means and how it guides decisions. Explain the belief or constraint in concrete terms, not just restate the title. Good descriptions answer: 'What does this look like in practice?' and 'How would I use this to resolve a disagreement?'

principles.description: Documents guiding principles for a visual style or design domain. Each item is a high-level belief or constraint that shapes decisions within this domain. Principles sit above individual best practices — they inform the philosophy, while best practices encode specific rules. A spacing style might have 'Use the scale' and 'Density over decoration'; a color style might have 'Functional first' and 'Accessible by default'.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a principles spec.

items.description: The guiding principles. Each item documents a single principle with a title and description. Ordering is significant — tools SHOULD display principles in this order. Authors SHOULD order by importance or by the natural progression of the design philosophy.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

scaleStep.description: A single step in an ordered scale. Each step references a design token that provides the concrete value for that position in the progression. The optional label, value, and description provide context for display without duplicating the token's authoritative definition.

token.description: The token identifier for this step. MUST reference a token defined in the token schema (e.g., 'space-4', 'font-size-300', 'elevation-low'). This is the single source of truth for the step's value — the optional `value` property is a display convenience only.

label.description: A human-readable label for this step (e.g., 'sm', 'md', 'lg', '2xl', 'body', 'heading-1'). Labels provide a shorthand vocabulary that designers and engineers use to communicate about scale positions without referring to raw values.

value.description: The resolved value for display (e.g., '16px', '1rem', '#1a1a1a', '400'). This is a docs convenience — the authoritative value lives in the referenced token. Tools MAY populate this from the token catalog.

description.description: Usage notes for this step — when to use it, what it pairs with, and any constraints (e.g., 'The base unit. Use for default internal component padding.', 'Reserved for page-level hero sections only.').

scale.description: Documents an ordered scale — a deliberate progression of values that constrains design decisions to a harmonious, consistent set. Scales are the primary mechanism for spacing systems, type hierarchies, elevation levels, and other graduated visual attributes. The steps array is ordered from smallest/lowest to largest/highest. Tools SHOULD preserve this ordering for display.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a scale spec.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier of the scale (e.g., 'spacing-scale', 'type-scale', 'elevation-scale'). Used for cross-referencing and tooling.

name.description: Human-readable name of the scale (e.g., 'Spacing Scale', 'Type Scale', 'Elevation Levels').

description.description: What this scale is, how it was constructed (e.g., geometric progression, modular scale), and the rules for using it. Document the scale's rationale and constraints — why these values were chosen and when deviation is appropriate.

steps.description: The ordered steps in the scale, from smallest/lowest to largest/highest. Ordering is significant — tools SHOULD preserve it for display. Consumers SHOULD treat adjacent steps as the natural next-smaller and next-larger options when adjusting a value.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

stateEntry.description: A single interactive state of a component. States represent transient or persistent visual/behavioral changes triggered by user interaction, system events, or data conditions.

identifier.description: Machine-readable state identifier (e.g., 'default', 'hover', 'focus', 'active', 'disabled', 'loading', 'selected', 'error', 'read-only').

description.description: What triggers this state, how appearance and behavior change, and any constraints or side effects.

tokens.description: Design token overrides applied when this state is active. Keys are token property names scoped to the component (e.g., 'button-background', 'button-border-color'). Values are the token identifiers or resolved values used in this state. Only tokens that change from the default state need to be listed. Note: these token references are descriptive summaries for designers. Authoritative resolved values for each variant × state combination live in the design-specifications guideline's overrides array.

purpose.description: Why this state exists — the user need or design rationale it addresses.

preview.description: Visual previews showing the component in this state.

states.description: Documents all interactive states of a component. Each item describes a single state — what triggers it, how appearance and behavior change, which tokens are overridden, and how it looks. States are listed in a meaningful order — typically default first, then hover, focus, active, followed by disabled, loading, and other special states.

kind.description: Identifies this guideline as a states spec.

items.description: The interactive states. Each item documents a single state with its trigger, visual changes, token overrides, and optional previews. Ordering is significant — tools SHOULD display states in this order.

agents.description: AI-ready context for this document block. Provides structured info for AI/LLM use: constraints, disambiguation, anti-patterns, examples, and keywords.

variantValue.description: A single option within an enum variant dimension.

identifier.description: Machine-readable value identifier (e.g., 'sm', 'md', 'lg', 'primary', 'secondary').

description.description: What this value looks like, when to use it, and how it differs from other values in this dimension.

purpose.description: Why this flag exists — the user need or design rationale it addresses (e.g., 'Prevents user interaction during form submission to avoid duplicate requests').

example.description: Visual previews showing this variant value in isolation or in context.

flagVariant.description: A boolean/toggle variant that is either on or off. Use for binary capabilities such as 'disabled', 'full-width', 'icon-only', or 'loading'. Flag variants have no values array — the component either has the flag active or not.

kind.description: Identifies this variant as a boolean flag.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier of the flag (e.g., 'disabled', 'full-width', 'icon-only', 'loading').

name.description: Human-readable name of the flag (e.g., 'Disabled', 'Full Width', 'Icon Only', 'Loading').

description.description: What this flag controls and how it affects the component's appearance or behavior when active.

purpose.description: Why this variant exists — the user need or design rationale it addresses (e.g., 'Prevents user interaction during form submission to avoid duplicate requests').

example.description: Visual preview showing the component with this flag active.

enumVariant.description: An enumerated variant with multiple mutually exclusive values. Use for dimensions where the user selects one option from a set, such as 'size' (sm | md | lg), 'emphasis' (primary | secondary | ghost), or 'shape' (rounded | pill | square).

kind.description: Identifies this variant as an enumerated set of values.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier of the variant dimension (e.g., 'size', 'emphasis', 'shape'). This is the axis name, not a value within it.

name.description: Human-readable name of the variant dimension (e.g., 'Size', 'Emphasis', 'Shape').

description.description: What this dimension of variation controls and how its values affect the component's appearance or behavior.
